title: A vault of seeds for a future nobody can see
summary: Argue whether it is worth spending money now to store millions of crop seeds in a frozen vault for emergencies that may never come, using present perfect, conditionals, and concession.
topic: Is it worth freezing millions of crop seeds for emergencies that may never happen?
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) arguing whether wealthy countries should keep funding a frozen seed vault that stores backup copies of the world's crop seeds, even though most of those seeds may never be needed. Weigh the cost of insurance against the cost of regret. Use present perfect at least once, a conditional at least once, and 'although' or 'however' at least once.
hintStarters: On a frozen island far from anywhere, engineers have built a vault that most people will never see, and yet…
Although the chance of needing these seeds in any single year is very small, the cost of being wrong…
If a war or a disease wiped out one country's harvest, the only surviving copy of those seeds might…

hintVocab.meaningVi: lưu trữ một bản sao dự phòng
tài trợ cho một dự án
cái giá của sự hối tiếc
chịu đựng được một cuộc khủng hoảng
một tổn thất không thể đảo ngược
nuôi sống một dân số đang tăng
nguồn cung lương thực toàn cầu

hintVocab.phrase: to weigh the cost against the benefit
hintVocab.meaningVi: cân nhắc chi phí so với lợi ích
một sự kiện có xác suất thấp
bảo tồn cho các thế hệ tương lai

sampleText:
Deep inside a mountain on a cold island near the North Pole, there is a long tunnel that leads to three frozen rooms. The rooms hold boxes, and the boxes hold envelopes, and the envelopes hold seeds — over a million different samples of the crops that the world eats. Most of the people who deposited these seeds will never come back for them. The vault is not a farm and not a museum. It is a backup copy of agriculture itself, kept frozen in case the original is ever damaged. Almost nobody will visit it this year, or next year, or for many years after that. And that, supporters say, is exactly the point.

I believe the vault is worth every coin it costs, and the reason is simple: it is an insurance policy, and insurance only looks expensive until the day you need it. A single country can lose its seed collection in an afternoon — to a flood, a fire, a war, or a broken freezer in an underfunded laboratory. This has already happened. A seed bank in Syria was abandoned during the fighting there, and the backup copies stored in the frozen vault were the only reason those varieties survived. If those copies had not existed, an entire library of drought-resistant crops would have vanished, and no amount of money could have bought it back. Some losses cannot be repaired afterwards, only prevented beforehand.

Although critics argue that the money would do more good if it were spent on hungry people today rather than on emergencies that may never come, I think this misreads what the vault is for. The annual cost of keeping the seeds frozen is tiny compared with the value of what they protect, and the two needs do not compete: feeding people now and protecting the food supply later are not the same budget. The deeper objection is really about how we think about risk. We are good at counting today's hunger and bad at imagining tomorrow's. If a new plant disease wiped out a major crop across several continents, the frozen samples might be the only place on earth where a resistant variety still existed. We would not be grateful for the money we saved by closing the vault. We would be asking, with no good answer, why we ever thought a low-probability disaster was the same thing as an impossible one. Keeping the vault open is not a bet that catastrophe will come. It is a refusal to gamble that it never will.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Third conditional for an avoided disaster
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If those copies had not existed, an entire library of drought-resistant crops would have vanished' dùng câu điều kiện loại 3 (if + past perfect, would have + V3) để nói về một điều KHÔNG xảy ra trong quá khứ và kết quả tưởng tượng của nó. Nó nhấn mạnh thảm họa đã suýt xảy ra nhưng đã được ngăn chặn.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If those copies had not existed, an entire library of drought-resistant crops would have vanished' uses the third conditional (if + past perfect, would have + past participle) to describe something that did NOT happen and its imagined result. It stresses a disaster that was narrowly avoided.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for a fact still relevant now
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'This has already happened' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành với 'already' để chỉ ra rằng sự việc đã xảy ra tại một thời điểm không xác định trong quá khứ nhưng vẫn có ý nghĩa cho lập luận hiện tại — nó chứng minh rủi ro là có thật, không phải giả thuyết.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'This has already happened' uses the present perfect with 'already' to show the event occurred at an unspecified past time but still matters to the present argument — it proves the risk is real, not hypothetical.

sampleTranslationVi:
Sâu bên trong một ngọn núi trên một hòn đảo lạnh giá gần Bắc Cực, có một đường hầm dài dẫn đến ba căn phòng đông lạnh. Các căn phòng chứa các hộp, các hộp chứa các phong bì, và các phong bì chứa hạt giống — hơn một triệu mẫu khác nhau của các loại cây trồng mà thế giới ăn. Hầu hết những người đã gửi lưu những hạt giống này sẽ không bao giờ quay lại lấy chúng. Hầm chứa không phải là một trang trại và cũng không phải một bảo tàng. Nó là một bản sao dự phòng của chính nền nông nghiệp, được giữ đông lạnh phòng trường hợp bản gốc có lúc nào đó bị hư hỏng. Gần như không ai sẽ ghé thăm nó trong năm nay, hay năm sau, hay nhiều năm sau đó nữa. Và đó, theo những người ủng hộ, chính xác là điểm mấu chốt.

Tôi tin rằng hầm chứa xứng đáng với từng đồng mà nó tiêu tốn, và lý do thì đơn giản: nó là một hợp đồng bảo hiểm, và bảo hiểm chỉ trông đắt đỏ cho đến ngày bạn cần đến nó. Một quốc gia có thể mất bộ sưu tập hạt giống của mình chỉ trong một buổi chiều — vì lũ lụt, hỏa hoạn, chiến tranh, hay một chiếc tủ đông hỏng trong một phòng thí nghiệm thiếu kinh phí. Điều này đã xảy ra rồi. Một ngân hàng hạt giống ở Syria đã bị bỏ rơi trong cuộc giao tranh ở đó, và các bản sao dự phòng được lưu trong hầm đông lạnh là lý do duy nhất khiến những giống đó sống sót. Nếu những bản sao đó không tồn tại, cả một thư viện cây trồng chịu hạn sẽ biến mất, và không số tiền nào có thể mua lại được. Một số tổn thất không thể sửa chữa về sau, chỉ có thể phòng ngừa từ trước.

Mặc dù những người chỉ trích lập luận rằng số tiền sẽ có ích hơn nếu được chi cho những người đói ngày hôm nay thay vì cho những trường hợp khẩn cấp có thể không bao giờ đến, tôi nghĩ điều này hiểu sai mục đích của hầm chứa. Chi phí hằng năm để giữ hạt giống đông lạnh là rất nhỏ so với giá trị của những gì chúng bảo vệ, và hai nhu cầu này không cạnh tranh với nhau: nuôi sống mọi người bây giờ và bảo vệ nguồn cung lương thực sau này không cùng một ngân sách. Phản đối sâu xa hơn thực ra là về cách chúng ta nghĩ về rủi ro. Chúng ta giỏi đếm cơn đói hôm nay nhưng kém trong việc hình dung cơn đói ngày mai. Nếu một bệnh thực vật mới xóa sổ một loại cây trồng chính trên nhiều châu lục, các mẫu đông lạnh có thể là nơi duy nhất trên trái đất mà một giống kháng bệnh vẫn còn tồn tại. Chúng ta sẽ không biết ơn số tiền tiết kiệm được nhờ đóng cửa hầm. Chúng ta sẽ tự hỏi, mà không có câu trả lời thỏa đáng, tại sao chúng ta lại từng nghĩ rằng một thảm họa xác suất thấp cũng giống như một thảm họa không thể xảy ra. Giữ cho hầm hoạt động không phải là một vụ cá cược rằng thảm họa sẽ đến. Đó là sự từ chối đánh cược rằng nó sẽ không bao giờ đến.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the third conditional to describe a disaster that was avoided?
options: If those copies had not existed, an entire library of crops would vanish.
If those copies did not exist, an entire library of crops would have vanished.
If those copies hadn't existed, an entire library of crops will have vanished.
If those copies had not existed, an entire library of crops would have vanished.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional = if + past perfect, would have + past participle, used for unreal past situations. 'Would vanish' mixes in a second-conditional result. 'Did not exist' is the second-conditional if-clause, not the past perfect. 'Will have vanished' uses a future form that cannot follow an unreal past condition.
mcQuestions.hint: Unreal past: if + had/hadn't + past participle, then would have + past participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the present perfect to show that a risk is real, not hypothetical?
options: This has already happened.
This already happened before now.
This has already happen.
This is already happened.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Has + past participle (happened)' is the correct present perfect and links a past event to the present argument. 'Already happened before now' is grammatical but wordy and unnatural. 'Has already happen' uses the base verb after 'has'. 'Is already happened' wrongly uses 'is' instead of 'has'.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect = has/have + past participle; the participle of 'happen' is 'happened'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly to concede the critics' point?
options: Although critics argue that the money could feed people today, but the vault protects the future.
Although critics argue that the money could feed people today, the vault protects the future.
Although critics argue that the money could feed people today the vault protects the future.
Critics argue that the money could feed people today, although however the vault protects the future.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, + main clause' is correct, with a comma and no extra connector. Option 2 adds a redundant 'but'. Option 3 omits the comma, making the sentence hard to read. Option 4 stacks 'although' and 'however' together.
mcQuestions.hint: Don't pair 'although' with 'but' or 'however'; use a comma between the clauses.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option most naturally describes the vault's purpose?
options: It is an insurance plan against an irreversible loss.
It is an insurance protection against an irreversible loss.
It is an insurance policy against an irreversible loss.
It is an insurance safety against an irreversible loss.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Insurance policy' is the natural, fixed collocation. 'Insurance plan' is used in some marketing contexts but is less precise here. 'Insurance protection' and 'insurance safety' are not standard English collocations.
mcQuestions.hint: Which two-word phrase do we normally use for a formal insurance agreement?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'compared with' to weigh cost against value?
options: The cost is tiny comparing with the value of what the seeds protect.
The cost is tiny compared the value of what the seeds protect.
The cost is tiny compared to with the value of what the seeds protect.
The cost is tiny compared with the value of what the seeds protect.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Compared with' is the standard form. 'Comparing with' wrongly uses the active participle for a passive idea. 'Compared the value' drops the preposition. 'Compared to with' doubles the preposition.
mcQuestions.hint: Use the past participle plus a single preposition: 'compared with'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a second conditional correctly to imagine a future emergency?
options: If a new disease wiped out a major crop, the frozen samples might be our only hope.
If a new disease wipes out a major crop, the frozen samples might be our only hope.
If a new disease wiped out a major crop, the frozen samples might to be our only hope.
If a new disease would wipe out a major crop, the frozen samples might be our only hope.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional = if + past simple, might/would + base verb, used for an unlikely future. Option 2 uses the present simple, making it a first conditional (more likely). Option 3 wrongly inserts 'to' after 'might'. Option 4 puts 'would' in the if-clause, which is non-standard.
mcQuestions.hint: Unlikely future: if + past simple, then might + base verb. No 'would' in the if-clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'no amount of' to stress that something cannot be recovered?
options: No amount of money could have bought back it.
No amount of money could have bought it back.
Not amount of money could have bought it back.
No amount of money could buying it back.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'No amount of money could have bought it back' is correct, with the pronoun 'it' placed before the particle 'back'. Option 2 puts the pronoun after 'back', which is wrong with a pronoun object. Option 3 uses 'not' instead of 'no'. Option 4 uses '-ing' after 'could'.
mcQuestions.hint: With phrasal verbs, a pronoun object goes before the particle: 'bought it back'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally expresses the contrast in how we judge risk?
options: We are good to count today's hunger and bad to imagine tomorrow's.
We are good at count today's hunger and bad at imagine tomorrow's.
We are good at counting today's hunger and bad at imagining tomorrow's.
We are good in counting today's hunger and bad in imagining tomorrow's.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Good at + gerund' is the correct pattern. Option 2 uses 'good to + infinitive', which means something different. Option 3 omits the '-ing' form after 'at'. Option 4 uses 'in' instead of 'at', which is unidiomatic with 'good'.
mcQuestions.hint: We are 'good at doing' something, not 'good in doing' or 'good to do'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'only ... afterwards / beforehand' to contrast prevention and repair?
options: Some losses cannot be repaired afterward, only prevent beforehand.
Some losses cannot repair afterwards, only prevented beforehand.
Some losses cannot be repair afterwards, only prevented beforehand.
Some losses cannot be repaired afterwards, only prevented beforehand.
mcQuestions.explanation: Both verbs are passive participles ('repaired', 'prevented') after the modal pattern, which is parallel and correct. Option 2 uses the base verb 'prevent'. Option 3 makes 'losses' the active subject of 'repair'. Option 4 drops the participle ending on 'repair'.
mcQuestions.hint: Keep the two verbs parallel and passive: 'cannot be repaired ... only prevented'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence is the clearest, most natural way to state the article's final point?
options: Keeping the vault open is not a bet that catastrophe will come; it is a refusal to gamble that it never will.
Keeping the vault open is not a bet that catastrophe will come, it is a refuse to gamble that it never will.
Keeping open the vault is not a bet that catastrophe will come; it is a refusal to gamble that it never will.
To keep the vault open is not a bet what catastrophe will come; it is a refusal to gamble that it never will.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option 1 is balanced, with the noun 'refusal' and a semicolon joining two related clauses. Option 2 uses the verb 'refuse' where a noun is needed and joins the clauses with only a comma. Option 3 says 'keeping open the vault', an awkward word order with this object. Option 4 uses 'what' instead of 'that' after 'bet'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the noun 'refusal' and correct linking; avoid the awkward word order and the wrong relative word.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The sample says we are good at counting today's hunger but bad at imagining tomorrow's disaster. Is it fair to spend money protecting future generations from emergencies while people are still suffering right now? How would you decide where one duty ends and the other begins?
